**Skateboarding** is an [action sport](/wiki/Extreme_sport "Extreme sport") that involves riding and [performing tricks](/wiki/Skateboarding_trick "Skateboarding trick") using a [skateboard](/wiki/Skateboard "Skateboard"), as well as a recreational activity, an art form, an entertainment industry [job](/wiki/Profession "Profession"), and a method of [transportation](/wiki/Transportation "Transportation").[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Originating in the United States, skateboarding has been shaped and influenced by many skateboarders throughout the years. A 2009 report found that the skateboarding market is worth an estimated $4.8 billion in annual revenue, with 11.08 million active skateboarders in the world.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 2016, it was announced that skateboarding would be represented at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), for both male and female teams.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Skateboarding made its Olympic debut in 2020 and was included in the 2024 games.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Since the 1970s, [skateparks](/wiki/Skatepark "Skatepark") have been constructed specifically for use by skateboarders, [freestyle BMXers](/wiki/Freestyle_BMX "Freestyle BMX"), [aggressive skaters](/wiki/Aggressive_inline_skating "Aggressive inline skating"), and more recently, [scooters](/wiki/Freestyle_scootering "Freestyle scootering").[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, skateboarding has become controversial in areas in which the activity, although legal, has damaged curbs, stoneworks, steps, benches, plazas, and parks.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[7]](#cite_note-skateboarding-7)

## History

### 1940s–1960s

The first skateboards started with wooden boxes, or boards, with [roller skate](/wiki/Roller_skates "Roller skates") wheels attached to the bottom. Crate [scooters](/wiki/Kick_scooter "Kick scooter") preceded skateboards, having a wooden crate attached to the nose (front of the board), which formed rudimentary handlebars.[[8]](#cite_note-Gnarly-8)[[9]](#cite_note-SBH-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Wave-10) The boxes turned into planks, similar to the skateboard decks of today.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)

Skateboarding, as it exists today, was probably born sometime in the late 1940s, or early 1950s,[[11]](#cite_note-11) when [surfers](/wiki/Surfers "Surfers") in [California](/wiki/California "California") wanted something to do when the waves were flat. This was called "sidewalk surfing" – a new wave of surfing on the sidewalk as the sport of surfing became highly popular. No one knows who made the first board; it seems that several people came up with similar ideas at around the same time. The first manufactured skateboards were ordered by a [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, surf shop, meant to be used by surfers in their downtime. The shop owner, Bill Richard, made a deal with the Chicago Roller Skate Company[[12]](#cite_note-12) to produce sets of skate wheels, which they attached to square wooden boards. Accordingly, skateboarding was originally denoted "sidewalk surfing" and early skaters emulated [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing") style and maneuvers, and performed barefoot.[[8]](#cite_note-Gnarly-8)[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeyland200221-13)

By the 1960s a small number of surfing manufacturers in [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California") such as Jack's, Kips', [Hobie](/wiki/Hobart_Alter "Hobart Alter"), Bing's and Makaha started building skateboards that resembled small surfboards, and assembled teams to promote their products. One of the earliest Skateboard exhibitions was [sponsored](/wiki/Skateboarding_sponsorship "Skateboarding sponsorship") by Makaha's founder, [Larry Stevenson](/wiki/Larry_Stevenson "Larry Stevenson"), in 1963 and it was held at the Pier Avenue Junior High School in [Hermosa Beach, California](/wiki/Hermosa_Beach,_California "Hermosa Beach, California").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-History-16) Some of these same teams of skateboarders were also featured on a television show called *Surf's Up* in 1964, hosted by Stan Richards, that helped promote skateboarding as something new and fun to do.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

As the popularity of skateboarding began expanding, the first skateboarding magazine, *[The Quarterly Skateboarder](/wiki/The_Quarterly_Skateboarder "The Quarterly Skateboarder")*, was published in 1964.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) [John Severson](/wiki/John_Severson "John Severson"), who published the magazine, wrote in his first editorial:

> Today's skateboarders are founders in this sport—they're pioneers—they are the first. There is no history in Skateboarding—its being made now—by you. The sport is being molded and we believe that doing the right thing now will lead to a bright future for the sport. Already, there are storm clouds on the horizon with opponents of the sport talking about ban and restriction.[[18]](#cite_note-writers-18)

The magazine only lasted four issues, but resumed publication as *[Skateboarder](/wiki/Skateboarder_(magazine) "Skateboarder (magazine)")* in 1975.[[18]](#cite_note-writers-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) The first broadcast of an actual skateboarding competition was the 1965 National Skateboarding Championships, which were held in [Anaheim](/wiki/Anaheim "Anaheim"), California and aired on ABC's *[Wide World of Sports](/wiki/Wide_World_of_Sports_(American_TV_program) "Wide World of Sports (American TV program)")*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Because skateboarding was a new sport during this time, there were only two original disciplines during competitions: flatland [freestyle](/wiki/Freestyle_skateboarding "Freestyle skateboarding") and [slalom](/wiki/Slalom_skateboarding "Slalom skateboarding") downhill racing.[[8]](#cite_note-Gnarly-8)

Animated cartoons of the time occasionally featured skateboard gags. Two [Road Runner](/wiki/Wile_E._Coyote_and_the_Road_Runner "Wile E. Coyote and the Road Runner") cartoons made in 1965, *[Shot and Bothered](/wiki/Shot_and_Bothered "Shot and Bothered")* and *[Out and Out Rout](/wiki/Out_and_Out_Rout "Out and Out Rout")*, feature Wile E. Coyote riding a skateboard.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

One of the earliest sponsored skateboarders, [Patti McGee](/wiki/Patti_McGee "Patti McGee"), was paid by Hobie and Vita Pak to travel around the country to do skateboarding exhibitions and to demonstrate skateboarding safety tips. McGee made the cover of *[Life](/wiki/Life_(magazine) "Life (magazine)")* magazine[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[25]](#cite_note-25) in 1965 and was featured on several popular television programs—*[The Mike Douglas Show](/wiki/The_Mike_Douglas_Show "The Mike Douglas Show")*, *[What's My Line?](/wiki/What%27s_My_Line%3F "What's My Line?")* and *[The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson](/wiki/The_Tonight_Show_Starring_Johnny_Carson "The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson")*—which helped make skateboarding even more popular at the time.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Some other well known surfer-style skateboarders of the time were Danny Bearer, Torger Johnson, Bruce Logan, Bill and Mark Richards, Woody Woodward, and Jim Fitzpatrick.

The growth of the sport during this period can also be seen in sales figures for Makaha, which quoted $4 million worth of board sales between 1963 and 1965.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeyland200228-29) By 1966 a variety of sources began to claim that skateboarding was dangerous, resulting in shops being reluctant to sell them, and parents being reluctant to buy them. In 1966 sales had dropped significantly[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeyland200228-29) and *[Skateboarder Magazine](/wiki/Skateboarder_(magazine) "Skateboarder (magazine)")* had stopped publication. The popularity of skateboarding dropped and remained low until the early 1970s.[[9]](#cite_note-SBH-9)[[30]](#cite_note-SBH2-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick-31)

### 1970s

In the early 1970s, [Frank Nasworthy](/wiki/Frank_Nasworthy "Frank Nasworthy") started to develop a skateboard wheel made of [polyurethane](/wiki/Polyurethane "Polyurethane"), calling his company Cadillac Wheels.[[9]](#cite_note-SBH-9) Prior to this new material, skateboards wheels were metal or "clay" wheels.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) The improvement in traction and performance was so immense that from the wheel's release in 1972 the popularity of skateboarding started to rise rapidly again, causing companies to invest more in product development. Nasworthy commissioned artist [Jim Evans](/wiki/Jim_Evans_(artist) "Jim Evans (artist)") to do a series of paintings promoting Cadillac Wheels, they were featured as ads and posters in the resurrected *Skateboarder Magazine*, and proved immensely popular in promoting the new style of skateboarding.

In the early 1970s, the precursors to the modern skateparks for skateboarding would be the repurposing of urban hydro and storm water infrastructure such as the [Escondido](/wiki/Escondido,_California "Escondido, California") reservoir in [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), California.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) *Skateboarding* magazine would publish the location and skateboarders made up nicknames for each location such as the Tea Bowl, the Fruit Bowl, Bellagio, the Rabbit Hole, Bird Bath, the Egg Bowl, Upland Pool and the Sewer Slide. Some of the development concepts in the terrain of skateparks were actually taken from the Escondido reservoir.[[32]](#cite_note-Stalefish-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Many companies started to manufacture trucks (axles) specially designed for skateboarding, reached in 1976 by Tracker Trucks. As the equipment became more maneuverable, the decks started to get wider, reaching widths of 10 inches (250 mm) and over, thus giving the skateboarder even more control.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) A banana board is a skinny, flexible skateboard made of [polypropylene](/wiki/Polypropylene "Polypropylene") with ribs on the underside for structural support. These were very popular during the mid-1970s and were available in a myriad of colors, bright yellow probably being the most memorable, hence the name.

In 1975, skateboarding had risen back in popularity enough to have one of the largest skateboarding competitions since the 1960s, the Del Mar National Championships, which is said to have had up to 500 competitors. The competition lasted two days and was sponsored by Bahne Skateboards and Cadillac Wheels. While the main event was won by freestyle spinning skate legend [Russ Howell](/wiki/Russ_Howell "Russ Howell"),[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) a local skate team from [Santa Monica](/wiki/Santa_Monica "Santa Monica"), California, the Zephyr team, ushered in a new era of surfer style skateboarding during the competition that would have a lasting impact on skateboarding's history. With a team of 12, including skating legends such as [Jay Adams](/wiki/Jay_Adams "Jay Adams"), [Tony Alva](/wiki/Tony_Alva "Tony Alva"), [Peggy Oki](/wiki/Peggy_Oki "Peggy Oki") and [Stacy Peralta](/wiki/Stacy_Peralta "Stacy Peralta"), they brought a new progressive style of skateboarding to the event, based on the style of Hawaiian surfers [Larry Bertlemann](/wiki/Bertlemann_slide "Bertlemann slide"), [Buttons Kaluhiokalani](/wiki/Buttons_Kaluhiokalani "Buttons Kaluhiokalani") and Mark Liddell.[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Craig Stecyk](/wiki/Craig_Stecyk "Craig Stecyk"), a photo journalist for *Skateboarder Magazine*, wrote about and photographed the team, along with [Glen E. Friedman](/wiki/Glen_E._Friedman "Glen E. Friedman"), and shortly afterwards ran a series on the team called the Dogtown articles, which eventually immortalized the Zephyr skateboard team. The team became known as the [Z-Boys](/wiki/Z-Boys "Z-Boys") and would go on to become one of the most influential teams in skateboarding's history.[[32]](#cite_note-Stalefish-32)[[38]](#cite_note-Legend-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Soon, skateboarding contests for cash and prizes, using a professional tier system, began to be held throughout California, such as the [California Free Former](/wiki/California_Free_Former "California Free Former") World Professional Skateboard Championships, which featured freestyle and slalom competitions.[[40]](#cite_note-Birthday-40)

A precursor to the extreme sport of [street luge](/wiki/Street_luge "Street luge"), that was sanctioned by the United States Skateboarding Association (USSA), also took place during the 1970s in [Signal Hill](/wiki/Signal_Hill,_California "Signal Hill, California"), California. The competition was called "The Signal Hill Skateboarding Speed Run", with several competitors earning entries into the *[Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records")*, at the time clocking speeds of over 50 mph (80 km/h) on a skateboard. Due to technology and safety concerns at the time, when many competitors crashed during their runs, the sport did not gain popularity or support during this time.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In March 1976, Skateboard City skatepark in [Port Orange](/wiki/Port_Orange "Port Orange"), Florida and Carlsbad Skatepark in [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County "San Diego County"), California would be the first two large size US skateparks to be opened to the public, just a week apart.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) They were the first of some 200 skateparks that would be built through 1982. This was due in part to articles that were running in the investment journals at the time, stating that skateparks were a good investment.[[8]](#cite_note-Gnarly-8)[[32]](#cite_note-Stalefish-32)[[43]](#cite_note-43) Notable skateboarders from the 1970s also include [Ty Page](/wiki/Ty_Page "Ty Page"), Tom Inouye, Laura Thornhill, Ellen O'Neal, Kim Cespedes, Bob Biniak, Jana Payne, Waldo Autry, Robin Logan, Bobby Piercy, Russ Howell, Ellen Berryman, [Shogo Kubo](/wiki/Shogo_Kubo "Shogo Kubo"), Desiree Von Essen, Henry Hester, Robin Alaway, Paul Hackett, Michelle Matta, Bruce Logan, Steve Cathey, Edie Robertson, Mike Weed, David Hackett, Gregg Ayres, Darren Ho, and [Tom Sims](/wiki/Tom_Sims "Tom Sims")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

Manufacturers started to experiment with more exotic composites and metals, like [fiberglass](/wiki/Fiberglass "Fiberglass") and [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum "Aluminum"), but the common skateboards were made of maple plywood.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) The skateboarders took advantage of the improved handling of their skateboards and started inventing new tricks. Skateboarders, most notably Ty Page, Bruce Logan, Bobby Piercy, Kevin Reed, and the Z-Boys started to skate the vertical walls of swimming pools that were left empty in the [1976 California drought](/wiki/Drought_in_the_United_States#Localized_United_States_droughts "Drought in the United States"). This started the "[vert](/wiki/Vert_(sport) "Vert (sport)")" trend in skateboarding. With increased control, vert skaters could skate faster and perform more dangerous tricks, such as slash grinds and frontside/backside airs. This caused liability concerns and increased insurance costs to skatepark owners, and the development (first by [Norcon](/w/index.php?title=Norcon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Norcon (page does not exist)"), then more successfully by Rector) of improved knee pads that had a hard sliding cap and strong strapping proved to be too-little-too-late. During this era, the "freestyle" movement in skateboarding began to splinter off and develop into a much more specialized discipline, characterized by the development of a wide assortment of flat-ground tricks.

As a result of the "vert" skating movement, skate parks had to contend with high liability costs that led to many park closures. In response, vert skaters started making their own ramps, while freestyle skaters continued to evolve their flatland style. Thus, by the beginning of the 1980s, skateboarding had once again declined in popularity.[[30]](#cite_note-SBH2-30)

### 1980s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Judi_Oyama_Rich_Oyama_Capitola_White_Shirt.png/250px-Judi_Oyama_Rich_Oyama_Capitola_White_Shirt.png)](/wiki/File:Judi_Oyama_Rich_Oyama_Capitola_White_Shirt.png)

[Judi Oyama](/wiki/Judi_Oyama "Judi Oyama") in 1980

This period was fueled by skateboard companies that were run by skateboarders. The focus was initially on [vert ramp](/wiki/Vert_ramp "Vert ramp") skateboarding. The invention of the no-hands aerial (later known as the [ollie](/wiki/Ollie_(skateboarding) "Ollie (skateboarding)")) by [Alan Gelfand](/wiki/Alan_Gelfand "Alan Gelfand") in Florida in 1976,[[44]](#cite_note-sm-44) and the almost parallel development of the grabbed aerial by George Orton and [Tony Alva](/wiki/Tony_Alva "Tony Alva") in California, made it possible for skaters to perform airs on vertical ramps. While this wave of skateboarding was sparked by commercialized vert ramp skating, a majority of people who skateboarded during this period did not ride vert ramps. As most people could not afford to build vert ramps, or did not have access to nearby ramps, [street skating](/wiki/Street_skateboarding "Street skateboarding") increased in popularity.

Freestyle skating remained healthy throughout this period, with pioneers such as [Rodney Mullen](/wiki/Rodney_Mullen "Rodney Mullen") inventing many of the basic tricks that would become the foundation of modern street skating, such as the "Impossible" and the "[kickflip](/wiki/Kickflip "Kickflip")".[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) The influence that freestyle exerted upon street skating became apparent during the mid-1980s; however, street skating was still performed on wide vert boards with short noses, slide rails, and large soft wheels. In response to the tensions created by this confluence of skateboarding "genres", a rapid evolution occurred in the late 1980s to accommodate the street skater. Since few skateparks were available to skaters at this time, street skating pushed skaters to seek out shopping centers and public and private property as their "spot" to skate. (Public opposition, in which businesses, governments, and property owners have banned skateboarding on properties under their jurisdiction or ownership, would progressively intensify over the following decades.) [[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) By 1992, only a small fraction of skateboarders continuing to take part in a highly technical version of street skating, combined with the decline of vert skating, produced a sport that lacked the mainstream appeal to attract new skaters.

During this period, numerous skateboarders—as well as companies in the industry—paid tribute to the scenes of [Marty McFly](/wiki/Marty_McFly "Marty McFly") skateboarding in the film *[Back to the Future](/wiki/Back_to_the_Future "Back to the Future")* for its influence in this regard. Examples can be seen in promotional material, in interviews in which professional skateboarders cite the film as an initiation into the action sport, and in the public's recognition of the film's influence.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Tony Hawk has stated that “there are plenty of legendary pros that I know of that started skating because they saw that [film].” [[49]](#cite_note-49)

### 1990s

Skateboarding during the 1990s became dominated by [street skateboarding](/wiki/Street_skateboarding "Street skateboarding").[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) Most boards are about 7+1⁄4 to 8 inches (180 to 200 mm) wide and 30 to 32 inches (760 to 810 mm) long. The wheels are made of an extremely hard [polyurethane](/wiki/Polyurethane "Polyurethane"), with hardness ([durometer](/wiki/Durometer "Durometer")) approximately 99A. The wheel sizes are relatively small so that the boards are lighter, and the wheels' inertia is overcome quicker, thus making tricks more manageable. Board styles have changed dramatically since the 1970s but have remained mostly alike since the mid-1990s. The contemporary shape of the skateboard is derived from the [freestyle](/wiki/Freestyle_skateboarding "Freestyle skateboarding") boards of the 1980s with a largely symmetrical shape and relatively narrow width. This form had become standard by the mid-1990s.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### 2000s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Bad-mama-jamas_0049.jpg/250px-Bad-mama-jamas_0049.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bad-mama-jamas_0049.jpg)

Skateboarder in [Manhattan, New York](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") (2008)

By 2001, skateboarding had gained so much popularity that more American people under the age of 18 rode skateboards (10.6 million) than played baseball (8.2 million), although traditional organized team sports still dominated youth programs overall.[[51]](#cite_note-jump-51) Skateboarding and skateparks began to be viewed and used in a variety of new ways to complement academic lessons in schools, including new non-traditional physical education skateboarding programs, like Skatepass[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[52]](#cite_note-52) and [Skateistan](/wiki/Skateistan "Skateistan"),[[53]](#cite_note-53) to encourage youth to have better attendance, self-discipline and confidence.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) This was also based on the healthy physical opportunities skateboarding was understood to bring participants for muscle & bone strengthening and balance, as well as the positive impacts it can have on youth in teaching them mutual respect, social networking, artistic expression and an appreciation of the environment.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In 2003, [Go Skateboarding Day](/wiki/Go_Skateboarding_Day "Go Skateboarding Day") was founded in southern California by the [International Association of Skateboard Companies](/wiki/International_Association_of_Skateboard_Companies "International Association of Skateboard Companies") (IASC)[[61]](#cite_note-61) to promote skateboarding throughout the world. It is celebrated annually on June 21 "to define skateboarding as the rebellious, creative celebration of independence it continues to be."[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)
According to market research firm American Sports Data the number of skateboarders worldwide increased by more than 60 percent between 1999 and 2002—from 7.8 million to 12.5 million.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Many cities also began implementing recreation plans and statutes during this time period, as part of their vision for local parks and communities to make public lands more available, in particular, for skateboarding, inviting skateboarders to come in off of the city streets and into organized skateboarding activity areas.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) By 2006, there were over 2,400 skateparks worldwide and the design of skateparks themselves had made a transition, as skaters turned designers.[[51]](#cite_note-jump-51)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-C4WDefault-5726471-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) Many new places to skateboard designed specifically for street skaters, such as [the Buszy](/wiki/The_Buszy "The Buszy") in [Milton Keynes](/wiki/Milton_Keynes "Milton Keynes"), UK, and the Safe Spot Skate Spot program, first initiated by professional skateboarder [Rob Dyrdek](/wiki/Rob_Dyrdek "Rob Dyrdek") throughout many cities, allowed for the creation of smaller alternative safe skate plazas to be built at a lower cost.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[72]](#cite_note-72) One of the largest locations ever built to skateboard in the world, [SMP Skatepark](/wiki/Skateboarding_in_China "Skateboarding in China") in China, at 12,000 square meters in size, was built complete with a 5,000-seat stadium.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In 2009, [Skatelab](/w/index.php?title=Skatelab&action=edit&redlink=1 "Skatelab (page does not exist)") opened the Skateboarding Hall of Fame & Skateboard Museum. Nominees are chosen by the IASC.[[74]](#cite_note-Six-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### 2010s–present

Downhill skateboarding (video) (2012)

Efforts have been taken to improve recognition of the cultural heritage as well as the positive effects of encouraging skateboarding within designated spaces. In 2015, the [John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), hosted an event at which skateboarders accompanied by music did tricks on a ramp constructed for a festival of [American culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_States "Culture of the United States").[[76]](#cite_note-76) The event was the climax of a ten-day project that transformed a federal institution formerly off-limits to the skateboarding community into a platform for that community to show its relevance through shared cultural action in a cultural common space.

By raising £790,000, the [Long Live Southbank](/wiki/Long_Live_Southbank "Long Live Southbank")[[77]](#cite_note-77) initiative managed in 2017 to curb the destruction of a forty year old spot in London, the [Southbank Undercroft](/wiki/Southbank_Centre "Southbank Centre"), a popular skate park, due to urban planning, a salvaging operation whose effect extends beyond skateboarding.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) The presence of a designated skating area within this public space keeps the space under nearly constant watch and drives [homeless people](/wiki/Homelessness_in_England "Homelessness in England") away, increasing the feeling of safety in and near the space.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The activity attracts artists such as photographers and film makers, as well as a significant number of tourists, which in turn drives economic activity in the neighborhood.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Recently, [barefoot](/wiki/Barefoot "Barefoot") skating has been experiencing a revival. Many skaters ride barefoot, particularly in summer and in warmer countries, such as [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"). The plastic [penny board](/wiki/Penny_board "Penny board") is intended to be ridden barefoot, as is the surfboard-inspired [hamboard](/wiki/Hamboard "Hamboard").

[Electric skateboards](/wiki/Electric_skateboard "Electric skateboard") became popular during the 2010s, as did [self-balancing unicycles](/wiki/Self-balancing_unicycle "Self-balancing unicycle") in a board format. The sport of skateboarding made its [Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") debut at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), with both men's and women's events. Competitions took place during July and August 2021 in two disciplines: street and park (see [Skateboarding at the 2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Skateboarding_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics "Skateboarding at the 2020 Summer Olympics")).[[80]](#cite_note-80)

## Trick skating

Main article: [Skateboarding trick](/wiki/Skateboarding_trick "Skateboarding trick")

With the evolution of [skateparks](/wiki/Skatepark "Skatepark") and ramp skating, the skateboard began to change. Early skate tricks had consisted mainly of two-dimensional [freestyle maneuvers](/wiki/Freestyle_skateboarding_tricks "Freestyle skateboarding tricks") like riding on only two wheels ("wheelie" or "manual"), spinning only on the back wheels (a "pivot"), high jumping over a bar and landing on the board again, also known as a "hippie jump", long jumping from one board to another, (often over small barrels or fearless teenagers), or slalom. Another popular trick was the [Bertlemann slide](/wiki/Bertlemann_slide "Bertlemann slide"), named after Larry Bertelemann's surfing maneuvers.

In 1976, skateboarding was transformed by the invention of the [ollie](/wiki/Ollie_(skateboarding) "Ollie (skateboarding)") by [Alan "Ollie" Gelfand](/wiki/Alan_Gelfand "Alan Gelfand").[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) It remained largely a unique Florida trick until the summer of 1978, when Gelfand made his first visit to California. Gelfand and his revolutionary maneuvers caught the attention of the West Coast skaters and the media where it began to spread worldwide. The ollie was adapted to flat ground by [Rodney Mullen](/wiki/Rodney_Mullen "Rodney Mullen") in 1982. Mullen also invented the "Magic Flip", which was later renamed the [kickflip](/wiki/Kickflip "Kickflip"), as well as many other tricks including the [360 Kickflip](/wiki/360_Kickflip "360 Kickflip"), which is a 360 pop [shove-it](/wiki/Shove-it "Shove-it") and a kickflip in the same motion. The flat ground ollie forms the basis of many street skating tricks, allowing skateboarders to perform tricks in mid-air without any more equipment than the skateboard itself. A recent development in the world of trick skating is the [1080](/wiki/1080_(skateboarding) "1080 (skateboarding)"), which was first ever landed by [Tom Schaar](/wiki/Tom_Schaar "Tom Schaar") in 2012.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### Film

Skateboarding was popularized by the 1986 skateboarding cult classic *[Thrashin'](/wiki/Thrashin%27_(film) "Thrashin' (film)")*. Directed by [David Winters](/wiki/David_Winters_(choreographer) "David Winters (choreographer)") and starring [Josh Brolin](/wiki/Josh_Brolin "Josh Brolin"), it features appearances from many famous skaters such as [Tony Alva](/wiki/Tony_Alva "Tony Alva"), [Tony Hawk](/wiki/Tony_Hawk "Tony Hawk"), [Christian Hosoi](/wiki/Christian_Hosoi "Christian Hosoi") and [Steve Caballero](/wiki/Steve_Caballero "Steve Caballero"). *Thrashin'* also had a direct impact on *[Lords of Dogtown](/wiki/Lords_of_Dogtown "Lords of Dogtown")*, as [Catherine Hardwicke](/wiki/Catherine_Hardwicke "Catherine Hardwicke"), who directed *Lords of Dogtown*, was hired by Winters to work on *Thrashin'* as a [production designer](/wiki/Production_designer "Production designer") where she met, worked with and befriended many famous skaters including the real Alva, Hawk, Hosoi and Caballero.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)

Skateboarding was, at first, tied to the culture of [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing"). As skateboarding spread across the United States to places unfamiliar with surfing or surfer culture, it developed an image of its own. For example, the classic film short *[Video Days](/wiki/Video_Days "Video Days")* (1991) portrayed skateboarders as "reckless rebels".[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)

California duo [Jan and Dean](/wiki/Jan_and_Dean "Jan and Dean") recorded the song "[Sidewalk Surfin'](/wiki/Sidewalk_Surfin%27 "Sidewalk Surfin'")" in 1964, which is the [Beach Boys](/wiki/Beach_Boys "Beach Boys") song "[Catch a Wave](/wiki/Catch_a_Wave "Catch a Wave")" with new lyrics associated with skateboarding instead of surfing.

### Skate parks

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Skate_park.webp/250px-Skate_park.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Skate_park.webp)

Kids playing on [scooters](/wiki/Freestyle_scootering "Freestyle scootering") at a [skatepark](/wiki/Skatepark "Skatepark")

Certain cities still oppose the building of [skate parks](/wiki/Skatepark "Skatepark") in their neighborhoods, for fear of increased crime and drugs in the area. The rift between the old image of skateboarding and a newer one is quite visible: magazines such as *[Thrasher](/wiki/Thrasher_(magazine) "Thrasher (magazine)")* portray skateboarding as dirty, rebellious, and still firmly tied to [punk](/wiki/Punk_subculture "Punk subculture"), while other publications, *[Transworld Skateboarding](/wiki/Transworld_Skateboarding "Transworld Skateboarding")* as an example, paint a more diverse and controlled picture of skateboarding. As more professional skaters use [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), or [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock") music accompaniment in their videos, many urban youths, hip hop fans, reggae fans, and hard rock fans are also drawn to skateboarding, further diluting the sport's punk image.

Group spirit supposedly influences the members of this community. In presentations of this sort, showcasing of criminal tendencies is absent, and no attempt is made to tie extreme sports to any kind of illegal activity. Female based skateboarding groups also exist, such as [Brujas](/wiki/Brujas_(skate_crew) "Brujas (skate crew)") which is based in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). Many women use their participation in skate crews to perform an alternative form of femininity.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[83]](#cite_note-83) These female skate crews offer a safe haven for women and girls in cities, where they can skate and bond without male expectations or competition.

### Video

The increasing availability of technology is apparent within the skateboarding community. Many skateboarders record and edit videos of themselves and friends skateboarding. However, part of this culture is to not merely replicate but to innovate; emphasis is placed on finding new places and landing new tricks.

### Video games

Further information: [List of skateboarding video games](/wiki/List_of_skateboarding_video_games "List of skateboarding video games")

Skateboarding video games have also become very popular in skateboarding culture.[[84]](#cite_note-Clayton-84) Some of the most popular are the [*Tony Hawk* series](/wiki/Tony_Hawk_(series) "Tony Hawk (series)") and *[Skate](/wiki/Skate_(2007_video_game) "Skate (2007 video game)")* series for various consoles (including hand-held) and personal computer.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Skate shoe

Further information: [Skate shoe](/wiki/Skate_shoe "Skate shoe")

Because early skateboarders were surfers trying to emulate the sport of surfing, many rode [barefoot](/wiki/Barefoot "Barefoot"), which led to foot injuries.[[31]](#cite_note-Fitzpatrick-31) This led to the need for a shoe that was specifically designed and marketed for skateboarding, such as the Randy "720", manufactured by the Randolph Rubber Company, and [Vans](/wiki/Vans "Vans") sneakers, which eventually became cultural iconic signifiers for skateboarders during the 1970s and '80s as skateboarding became more widespread.[[10]](#cite_note-Wave-10)[[87]](#cite_note-Popular-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) One of the early leading trends associated with the [sub-culture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture") of skateboarding itself was the sticky-soled slip-on [skate shoe](/wiki/Skate_shoe "Skate shoe"), most popularized by [Sean Penn](/wiki/Sean_Penn "Sean Penn")'s skateboarding character from the 1982 film *[Fast Times at Ridgemont High](/wiki/Fast_Times_at_Ridgemont_High "Fast Times at Ridgemont High")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[10]](#cite_note-Wave-10)

While the skate shoes design afforded better connection and traction with the deck, skateboarders themselves could often be identified when wearing the shoes, with Tony Hawk once saying, "If you were wearing Vans shoes in 86, you were a skateboarder".[[32]](#cite_note-Stalefish-32) Because of its connection with skateboarding, Vans financed the legendary skateboarding documentary *[Dogtown and Z-Boys](/wiki/Dogtown_and_Z-Boys "Dogtown and Z-Boys")* and was the first sneaker company to endorse a professional skateboarder, [Stacy Peralta](/wiki/Stacy_Peralta "Stacy Peralta"). Vans has a long history of being a major sponsor of many skateboarding competitions and events throughout skateboarding history as well, including the [Vans Warped Tour](/wiki/Warped_Tour "Warped Tour") and the Vans Triple Crown Series.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[10]](#cite_note-Wave-10)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-shoes-94)[[95]](#cite_note-Footwear-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)

As it eventually became more apparent that skateboarding had a particular identity with a style of shoe, other brands of shoe companies began to specifically design skate shoes for functionality and style to further enhance the experience and culture of skateboarding including such brands as; [Converse](/wiki/Converse_(shoe_company) "Converse (shoe company)"), [Nike](/wiki/Nike_Skateboarding "Nike Skateboarding"), [DC Shoes](/wiki/DC_Shoes "DC Shoes"), [Globe](/wiki/Globe_International "Globe International"), [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas"), [Zoo York](/wiki/Zoo_York_(company) "Zoo York (company)") and [World Industries](/wiki/World_Industries "World Industries"). Many professional skateboarders are designed a pro-model skate shoe, with their name on it, once they have received a [skateboarding sponsorship](/wiki/Skateboarding_sponsorship "Skateboarding sponsorship") after becoming notable skateboarders. Some shoe companies involved with skateboarding, like [Sole Technology](/wiki/Sole_Technology "Sole Technology"), an American footwear company that makes the [Etnies](/wiki/Etnies "Etnies") skate shoe brand, further distinguish themselves in the market by collaborating with local cities to open public [skateparks](/wiki/Skateparks "Skateparks"), such as the [etnies Skatepark](/wiki/Etnies_Skatepark "Etnies Skatepark") in [Lake Forest](/wiki/Lake_Forest,_California "Lake Forest, California"), California.[[94]](#cite_note-shoes-94)[[95]](#cite_note-Footwear-95)[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-Street-98)

### Skateboard deck

Further information: [Skateboard deck](/wiki/Skateboard_deck "Skateboard deck")

Individuality and a self-expressed casual style have always been cultural values for skateboarders, as uniforms and jerseys are not typically worn.[[99]](#cite_note-Ethnographic-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100) This type of personal style for skateboarders is often reflected in the graphical designs illustrated on the bottom of the deck of skateboards, since its initial conception in the mid-seventies, when Wes Humpston and Jim Muri first began doing design work for Dogtown Skateboards out of their garage by hand, creating the very first iconic skateboard-deck art with the design of the "Dogtown Cross".[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)

Prior to the mid-seventies many early skateboards were originally based upon the concept of “Sidewalk Surfing” and were tied to the [surf culture](/wiki/Surf_culture "Surf culture"), skateboards were surfboard like in appearance with little to no graphics located under the bottom of the skateboard-deck.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) Some of the early manufactured skateboards such as "Roller Derby", the "Duraflex Surfer" and the "Banana board" are characteristic. Some skateboards during that time were manufactured with company logo's or stickers across the top of the deck of the skateboard, as [griptape](/wiki/Griptape "Griptape") was not initially used for construction. But as skateboarding progressed and evolved, and as artists began to design and add influence to the artwork of skateboards, designs and themes began to change.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

There were several artistic skateboarding pioneers that had an influence on the culture of skateboarding during the 1980s, that transformed skateboard-deck art like Jim Phillips, whose edgy comic-book style "Screaming Hand", not only became the main logo for [Santa Cruz Skateboards](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_Skateboards "Santa Cruz Skateboards"), but eventually transcended into tattoos of the same image for thousands of people and vinyl collectible figurines over the years.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107) Artist Vernon Courtlandt Johnson is said to have used his artwork of skeletons and skulls, for [Powell Peralta](/wiki/Powell_Peralta "Powell Peralta"), during the same time that the music genres of [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and [new wave music](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") were beginning to mesh with the culture of skateboarding.[[10]](#cite_note-Wave-10)[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109) Some other notable skateboard artists that made contributions to the culture of skateboarding also include Andy Jenkins, [Todd Bratrud](/wiki/Todd_Bratrud "Todd Bratrud"), [Neil Blender](/wiki/Neil_Blender "Neil Blender"), [Marc McKee](/wiki/Marc_McKee "Marc McKee"), [Tod Swank](/wiki/Tod_Swank "Tod Swank"), [Mark Gonzales](/wiki/Mark_Gonzales "Mark Gonzales"), [Lance Mountain](/wiki/Lance_Mountain "Lance Mountain"), [Natas Kaupas](/wiki/Natas_Kaupas "Natas Kaupas") and [Jim Evans](/wiki/Jim_Evans_(artist) "Jim Evans (artist)").[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Over the years skateboard-deck art has continued to influence and expand the culture of skateboarding, as many people began collecting skateboards based on their artistic value and nostalgia. Productions of limited editions with particular designs and types of collectible prints that can be hung on the wall, have been created by such famous artists as [Andy Warhol](/wiki/Andy_Warhol "Andy Warhol") and [Keith Haring](/wiki/Keith_Haring "Keith Haring").[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1) Most professional skateboarders today have their own signature skateboard decks, with their favorite artistic designs printed on them using [computer graphics](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics").[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-Workshop-113)

### High value and collectible skateboards

In January 2019, [Sotheby's](/wiki/Sotheby%27s "Sotheby's") in New York auctioned[[114]](#cite_note-114) the full set of the 248 skateboard deck designs ever sold by [Supreme](/wiki/Supreme_(brand) "Supreme (brand)"), collected by Ryan Fuller. The full set sold for $800,000 to 17 year old Carson Guo from Vancouver[[115]](#cite_note-115) who plans to exhibit them in a local gallery.

New York based SHUT Skateboards had a goldplated skateboard for sale at $15,000 in 2014, then the most expensive skateboard in the world.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

In 2019, artist [Adrian Wilson](/wiki/Adrian_Wilson_(artist) "Adrian Wilson (artist)") created the SUPREME Mundi, a cross between an artist palette and a skateboard as a commentary on the record bids at auction of the Supreme decks and the restored *[Salvator Mundi](/wiki/Salvator_Mundi_(Leonardo) "Salvator Mundi (Leonardo)")* which was sold by a New York art gallery for $20,000[[117]](#cite_note-117)

## Safety

Skateboards, along with other small-wheeled transportation such as in-line skates and scooters, suffer a safety problem: riders may easily be thrown from small cracks and outcroppings in pavement, especially where the cracks run across the direction of travel. Hitting such an irregularity is the major cause of falls and injuries.[[118]](#cite_note-Fountain-1996-118) The risk may be reduced at higher travel speeds.

Severe injuries are relatively rare.[[119]](#cite_note-Keilani-2010-119) Commonly, a skateboarder who falls suffers from [scrapes](/wiki/Abrasion_(medical) "Abrasion (medical)"), [cuts](/wiki/Lacerations "Lacerations"), [bruises](/wiki/Bruise "Bruise"), and [sprains](/wiki/Sprain "Sprain").[[119]](#cite_note-Keilani-2010-119) Among injuries reported to a hospital, about half involve broken bones, usually the long bones in the leg or arm.[[118]](#cite_note-Fountain-1996-118) One third of skateboarders with reported injuries are very new to the sport, having started skating within one week of the injury.[[118]](#cite_note-Fountain-1996-118) Although less common, involving 3.5–9 percent of reported injuries, [traumatic head injuries](/wiki/Traumatic_head_injuries "Traumatic head injuries") and death are possible severe outcomes.[[118]](#cite_note-Fountain-1996-118)

Skating as a form of transportation exposes the skateboarder to the dangers of other traffic. Skateboarders on the street may be hit by other vehicles or may fall into vehicular traffic.

Skateboarders also occasionally pose a risk to other pedestrians and traffic. If the skateboarder falls, the skateboard may roll or fly into another person. A skateboarder who collides with a person who is walking or biking may injure or, rarely, kill that person.[[120]](#cite_note-White_2011_a887-120)

Many jurisdictions require skateboarders to wear [bicycle helmets](/wiki/Bicycle_helmet "Bicycle helmet") to reduce the risk of head injuries and death. Other protective gear, such as [wrist guards](/wiki/Wrist_guard "Wrist guard"), also reduce injury. Some medical researchers have proposed restricting skateboarding to designated, specially designed areas, to reduce the number and severity of injuries, and to eliminate injuries caused by motor vehicles or to other pedestrians.[[118]](#cite_note-Fountain-1996-118)

The use, ownership and sale of skateboards were forbidden in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") from 1978 to 1989 because of the high number of injuries caused by boards. The ban led skateboarders to construct ramps in the forest and other secluded areas to avoid the police. There was, however, one legal skatepark in the country in [Frogner Park](/wiki/Frogner_Park "Frogner Park") in Oslo.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123)

## Other uses and styles

For styles of skateboarding, see [Skateboarding styles](/wiki/Skateboarding_styles "Skateboarding styles").

### Transportation

The use of skateboards solely as a form of transportation is often associated with the [longboard](/wiki/Longboard_(skateboard) "Longboard (skateboard)").[[124]](#cite_note-124) Depending on local laws, using skateboards as a form of transportation outside residential areas may or may not be legal.[[125]](#cite_note-125) Backers cite portability, exercise, and environmental friendliness as some of the benefits of skateboarding as an alternative to automobiles.

### Military

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/USMC_with_a_skateboard.jpg/250px-USMC_with_a_skateboard.jpg)](/wiki/File:USMC_with_a_skateboard.jpg)

Soldier carrying a skateboard during a military exercise in [Oakland, California](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California") (March 1999)

The [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") tested the usefulness of commercial off-the-shelf skateboards during [urban combat](/wiki/Urban_warfare "Urban warfare") [military exercises](/wiki/Military_exercise "Military exercise") in the late 1990s in a program called Urban Warrior '99. Their special purpose was "for maneuvering inside buildings in order to detect [tripwires](/wiki/Tripwire "Tripwire") and [sniper](/wiki/Sniper "Sniper") fire".[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127)

### Trampboarding

Trampboarding is a variant of skateboarding that uses a board without the trucks and the wheels on a [trampoline](/wiki/Trampoline "Trampoline"). Using the bounce of the trampoline gives height to perform tricks, whereas in skateboarding one needs to make the height by performing an [ollie](/wiki/Ollie_(skateboarding) "Ollie (skateboarding)"). Trampboarding is seen on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") in numerous videos.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### Swing boarding

Swing boarding is the activity where a skateboard deck is suspended from a pivot point above the rider which allows the rider to swing about that pivot point. The board swings in an arc which is a similar movement to riding a half pipe. The incorporation of a harness and frame allows the rider to perform turns and spins all while flying through the air.

## Controversy

Skateboarding damages urban terrain features such as curbs, benches, and ledges when skateboarders perform "[grinds](/wiki/Grind_(skateboarding) "Grind (skateboarding)")" and other tricks on these surfaces.[[129]](#cite_note-Atlantic-129) Private industry has responded to this problem by using [skate deterrent devices](/wiki/Anti-skate_device "Anti-skate device"), such as the [Skatestopper](/wiki/Skatestopper "Skatestopper"), in efforts to prevent further damage and to reduce skateboarding on these surfaces.[[129]](#cite_note-Atlantic-129)

The enactment of ordinances and the posting of signs stating "Skateboarding is not allowed" have also become common methods to discourage skateboarding in public areas in many cities, to protect pedestrians and property.[[1]](#cite_note-Architecture-1)[[130]](#cite_note-Costello-130) In the area of street skating, tickets and arrest from police for [trespassing](/wiki/Trespass "Trespass") and [vandalism](/wiki/Vandalism "Vandalism") are not uncommon.[[130]](#cite_note-Costello-130)

Skateboarding has become an important problem in [Freedom Plaza](/wiki/Freedom_Plaza "Freedom Plaza"), a National Park within the [Pennsylvania Avenue National Historic Site](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Avenue_National_Historic_Site "Pennsylvania Avenue National Historic Site") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[7]](#cite_note-skateboarding-7)[[131]](#cite_note-Historic_Site-131) The Plaza has become a popular location for skateboarding, although the activity is illegal and has resulted in police actions.[[7]](#cite_note-skateboarding-7)[[132]](#cite_note-132) The Plaza contains copies of portions of [Pierre (Peter) Charles L'Enfant's 1791 plan for the nation's capital city](/wiki/L%27Enfant_Plan "L'Enfant Plan") that have been inscribed in the park's raised marble surface.[[131]](#cite_note-Historic_Site-131) A 2016 [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") management plan for the Historic Site states that skateboarding has damaged stonework, sculptures, walls, benches, steps, and other surfaces in some areas of the Plaza.[[7]](#cite_note-skateboarding-7) The management plan further states that skateboarding presents a persistent law enforcement and management challenge, as popular websites advertise the Plaza's attractiveness for the activity.[[7]](#cite_note-skateboarding-7) The plan notes that vandals have removed "No Skateboarding" signs and recommends the replacement of those signs.[[7]](#cite_note-skateboarding-7)

A professional skateboarder promoted on [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") the use of governmental sites for the prohibited activity during the [2013 federal government shutdown](/wiki/United_States_federal_government_shutdown_of_2013 "United States federal government shutdown of 2013") in the United States.[[133]](#cite_note-133)
